content.text: What Is an Approved LLM SDK and Framework Dependency Policy

An approved LLM SDK and framework dependency policy defines which third-party libraries, SDKs, and orchestration frameworks a team may use when building agentic AI systems—and the process by which new libraries may be proposed, evaluated, and approved. This policy exists because LLM client libraries and agent orchestration frameworks occupy a uniquely sensitive position in the dependency graph: they mediate all communication with AI model providers, process untrusted content from external sources, and in many frameworks execute arbitrary tool calls on behalf of the agent. A vulnerability or supply chain compromise in these libraries has broader impact than a vulnerability in a peripheral utility library.

The policy is typically implemented as a YAML file (`deps-policy.yaml` or similar) checked into the repository, listing approved packages with version constraints, enforcement scripts that compare the project's lock file against the approved list, and a pull request process for adding new entries. Organizations with multiple agent teams often manage the policy centrally at the platform team level, publishing it as a versioned internal package that teams import and extend with service-specific additions.
Why This Policy Matters

The LLM SDK ecosystem is young and evolving rapidly. Major frameworks like LangChain, LlamaIndex, and AutoGen have released breaking changes across minor versions; security vulnerabilities have been discovered in several widely-used LLM client libraries; and the supply chain risk—malicious packages with names similar to popular libraries—is heightened by the high search traffic and rapid adoption patterns in the AI tooling space. Without a curated approved list, developers may unknowingly add a package that duplicates an already-approved library (increasing attack surface without benefit), introduces a license incompatibility, or carries a known vulnerability that the security team has already assessed.

Consistent use of approved libraries also enables organizational investment in tooling and expertise. If the organization has standardized on the Anthropic Python SDK and LangChain Core, the platform team can build centralized wrappers, testing utilities, observability plugins, and hardened configuration defaults that work across all agent services. Fragmentation across five different LLM client libraries means five times the maintenance burden and no opportunity for shared tooling investment.
Core Benefits
Reduces supply chain risk by limiting the LLM library attack surface to a curated, security-reviewed set.
Enables centralized tooling investment: one approved client library means one place to add telemetry, rate limiting, and retry logic.
Prevents license incompatibilities from reaching production by evaluating license terms during approval, not at legal review.
Provides a clear upgrade path: the approved list specifies minimum versions that include necessary security patches.
Creates accountability: unapproved dependencies trigger a CI block that forces a documented approval decision.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Policy maintenance overhead: the approved list must be updated as new library versions ship and old versions reach end-of-life.
Risk of over-restriction: an overly conservative policy may block genuinely useful libraries, slowing delivery.
Approval latency: a 5-business-day review SLA can block fast-moving teams that need a new library urgently.
The policy covers declared dependencies but not transitive dependencies—a transitively pulled library may carry risk the policy does not directly address.
Policy files can become stale if the review process is not routinely exercised; annual re-evaluation cadence is the minimum.
How an Approved Dependency Policy Works

The policy defines three categories of dependencies. Approved dependencies are listed by package name, approved version range, and the use case they are approved for. Conditionally approved dependencies are allowed only in specific contexts or with specific configuration requirements (for example, a library approved only for internal services, not for internet-facing services). Blocked dependencies are explicitly denied and the block reason is documented (known vulnerability, license incompatibility, or functional overlap with an already-approved library that creates duplication risk).

CI enforcement reads the project's package lock file (requirements.txt, poetry.lock, package-lock.json) and compares each declared dependency against the approved list. If a package is not on the approved list, the CI step fails with a message identifying the package and linking to the approval request process. If a package is on the approved list but the declared version falls outside the approved range, the CI step fails with a message identifying the version constraint violation. This prevents developers from upgrading to a version not yet security-reviewed or downgrading below the minimum version that includes a required security patch.

The approval request process is a pull request against the policy repository. The PR template prompts the requester to document: the library name and version range being requested, the use case justification, the license terms, the OSSF Scorecard score for the library, any known vulnerabilities and their remediation status, and the alternatives considered. The platform security team and architecture team review the PR and either approve, reject with documented alternatives, or request additional information.
Architecture

attrs.alt: LLM SDK dependency approval lifecycle: from request PR through automated checks, human review, to allowlist and CI enforcement

content.text: When to Apply This Policy

Apply this policy from the first commit of any agent system that uses third-party LLM libraries. Retrofitting a dependency policy to an existing codebase that has already accumulated unlisted dependencies is more expensive than establishing it at the start. For new projects, the approved list serves as a scaffold: developers know which libraries are available and pre-approved, reducing the time spent evaluating options.

Review and update the policy on two triggers: scheduled (quarterly re-evaluation of every approved library for new vulnerabilities and version updates) and event-driven (a new CVE affecting an approved library, a major version release with breaking changes, or a library reaching end-of-life). Event-driven updates should have a faster SLA than scheduled reviews—a critical CVE in an approved library should trigger a policy update within 24 hours specifying the minimum safe version.
How to Implement

# deps-policy.yaml — checked into platform/policies repository
schema_version: "1.0"

approved:
  - package: "openai"
    language: python
    min_version: "1.30.0"
    approved_use: "Direct OpenAI API calls via provider adapter"
    license: MIT
    approved_date: "2024-09-01"
    reviewer: "@platform/security"

  - package: "anthropic"
    language: python
    min_version: "0.28.0"
    approved_use: "Anthropic Claude API calls via provider adapter"
    license: MIT
    approved_date: "2024-09-01"
    reviewer: "@platform/security"

  - package: "langchain-core"
    language: python
    min_version: "0.2.0"
    max_version: "<0.4.0"
    approved_use: "Agent orchestration abstractions only (not langchain-community)"
    license: MIT
    approved_date: "2024-11-15"
    reviewer: "@platform/architecture"

blocked:
  - package: "langchain-community"
    language: python
    reason: "Broad dependency surface; use approved alternatives for specific integrations"
    alternative: "Use individual provider packages directly"

  - package: "openai-unofficial"
    language: python
    reason: "Unofficial package with supply chain risk"
    alternative: "Use openai >= 1.30.0 (official package)"
Worked Examples

Example 1 — New library approval: A developer wants to use `llama-index-core` for its document indexing capabilities. They submit a PR to the policy repository with: OSSF Scorecard score of 7.2/10, license MIT, no known critical CVEs, use case 'RAG pipeline document chunking and indexing', and alternatives considered (LangChain document loaders — less flexible for multi-format documents). The platform team reviews, approves with a minimum version of 0.10.0 that includes a security fix for a prompt injection in metadata handling, and merges the policy PR. The developer's application PR passes CI after updating to ≥0.10.0.

Example 2 — CVE triggers emergency policy update: A critical CVE (CVSS 9.1) is disclosed in `langchain-core < 0.2.5` allowing prompt injection through malformed tool responses. The security team updates the policy within four hours, raising the minimum approved version to 0.2.5. An automated notification is sent to all teams using the affected package. Teams have 48 hours to update their lock files and pass CI before the old version is added to the blocked list.

Example 3 — Blocked package detection: A new team member adds `langchain-community` to requirements.txt, unaware of the policy. The CI dependency check step fails with the message 'BLOCKED: langchain-community — Broad dependency surface; use approved alternatives for specific integrations. Alternative: use individual provider packages directly.' The developer reviews the blocked list, identifies the specific integration they need (Redis cache), finds the standalone `langchain-redis` package which is on the approved list, and replaces the community package.
Real-World Application

A financial services platform team managing 12 internal agent services implemented a centralized dependency policy after a security incident where one team added an unofficial OpenAI client wrapper that contained a logging hook that exfiltrated prompts. After the incident, the team established a deps-policy.yaml managed in the platform repository, with CI enforcement running as a required check on all agent service PRs. In the first quarter, the CI gate blocked 23 unapproved dependency additions across the 12 services. Of those, 8 were approved after review, 11 were replaced with already-approved alternatives, and 4 were withdrawn because review revealed they were not actually needed. The platform team estimates the gate prevented at least two additional supply chain incidents similar to the one that prompted the policy.
Common Mistakes
Setting the policy at exact versions (`==1.30.0`) rather than minimum versions (`>=1.30.0`), blocking security patch upgrades without re-approval.
Approving packages at the namespace level (`langchain`) rather than sub-package level (`langchain-core`), inadvertently approving the entire ecosystem.
Not tracking transitive dependencies: a dependency of an approved package may introduce an unapproved library transitively.
Failing to remove deprecated or EOL packages from the approved list, keeping vulnerable packages appearing safe.
No emergency CVE process, causing teams to wait five business days for a critical security patch approval.
Best Practices
Scope the approved list to sub-packages (langchain-core, langchain-openai) not meta-packages, to limit inadvertent scope expansion.
Include OSSF Scorecard scores in the approval record and set a minimum score threshold (e.g., 6.0/10) for new approvals.
Generate a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for each agent service deployment and archive it alongside the deployment artifact.
Automate CVE scanning (Snyk, Grype, pip-audit) daily against approved packages and trigger emergency policy updates on CVSS ≥8.0 findings.
Document the approved use case for each library—this prevents scope creep where a library approved for one purpose is used in a different, riskier context.
Performance and Scalability

Dependency policy checks are fast—typically under 10 seconds—and should be one of the first CI steps, failing fast before more expensive checks run. For monorepo architectures with many agent services, run the check per service directory using matrix jobs, checking each service's lock file against the shared policy. The policy YAML file is small and can be fetched from the policy repository as a CI artifact, avoiding the need to version-bump every consuming repository each time the policy updates.

As the number of approved packages grows past 50–100 entries, consider a structured metadata schema and tooling to query the policy—for example, 'list all approved packages with known vulnerabilities in their currently approved version range'. A queryable policy database enables proactive monitoring rather than reactive CVE response.

Security and Reliability Considerations

The policy YAML file itself is a security artifact. Protect it with branch rules requiring two reviewers (one from security, one from architecture) for any change. Use signed commits on the policy repository so changes cannot be forged. Monitor the policy file for unauthorized modifications by comparing the deployed hash against the repository hash in each CI run.

Consider pin-hashing critical packages: instead of specifying a version range, specify the exact SHA256 hash of the approved release artifact. Hash pinning prevents dependency confusion attacks where a higher version of a package is published by a malicious actor to a private registry. This is particularly valuable for the LLM client libraries that have direct access to model provider APIs and may process sensitive prompts. Hash pinning adds maintenance overhead (hashes must be updated with each approved upgrade) but provides the strongest supply chain guarantee.
Managing Policy Across Multiple Teams and Repositories

For organizations with more than two or three agent teams, a centralized policy repository managed by the platform team is more sustainable than per-repository policy files. The central policy repository is the single source of truth; individual service repositories pull the policy as a versioned artifact during CI. When the platform team updates the policy (new approvals, version constraint changes, CVE responses), all consuming services automatically pick up the update on their next CI run without requiring a PR to each service repository.

Version the policy itself: tag each release with a semantic version and maintain a CHANGELOG documenting why each change was made. Services can pin to a specific policy version for stability while migration to a new policy version is planned. When the platform team publishes a policy that removes a previously approved package, services have a documented migration window (typically 30–60 days) to replace the dependency before the old version moves to the blocked list. Communicate policy version changes through the standard internal engineering announcement channels—team Slack channels, weekly engineering digest, architecture office hours.

For acquisitions or large-scale migrations where a new team joins the organization with existing dependencies, create an onboarding exception mechanism. A new team may have up to 20 existing unlisted dependencies that need approval. Grant a 90-day onboarding exception that lists these dependencies as conditionally approved, with a commitment to complete formal review within the window. Track the exception review queue as a work item in the platform team's backlog. This approach balances the need for immediate policy compliance with the practical reality that large dependency audits take time.
Handling AI-Specific Library Risks

LLM-specific libraries carry risks that generic security scoring tools may not capture. A library's OSSF Scorecard score measures supply chain hygiene (signed releases, maintained code, responsive to CVEs) but does not evaluate AI-specific risks: does the library handle model responses securely? Does it implement any output sanitization? Does it expose prompt content through logging or telemetry by default? These questions require manual review as part of the approval process for LLM client libraries and orchestration frameworks.

When reviewing an LLM framework for approval, evaluate three AI-specific dimensions. First, prompt handling: does the library log prompts to stdout or an external service by default? Prompt logging is often desirable for debugging but must be configurable and must respect data classification policies. Second, output parsing: does the library parse model outputs in ways that could execute code or process untrusted content unsafely? Frameworks with plugin systems that execute tool calls based on model output are higher risk and require additional scrutiny. Third, context contamination: does the library implement any context caching or sharing across requests that could inadvertently mix contexts from different users or sessions? Document the findings of each dimension in the approval record.
Policy Enforcement in Development Environments

Policy enforcement should apply in development environments as well as CI, but with a softer failure mode. In development, a dependency policy check failure should warn the developer and provide a link to the approval process—it should not prevent running the code locally, which would obstruct development flow. Reserve hard failures (exit code 1) for CI and pre-commit hooks that run before pushing. The practical implementation is a pip-audit wrapper script that runs with `--warn-only` in development mode and `--fail-on-violation` in CI mode, toggled by an environment variable.

Pre-commit hooks provide a middle ground between local development and CI enforcement. Configure a pre-commit hook that runs the dependency check before every commit—if the check detects a new unapproved package in requirements.txt, it prompts the developer to either submit an approval request or replace the dependency before committing. This catches violations at the developer's machine before they reach CI, reducing wasted CI minutes and giving faster feedback. The pre-commit hook should link directly to the policy repository's PR template so developers can immediately start the approval process from the error message.
Dependency Policy for Agent Tools and Plugins

Agent systems often extend their capability through tool plugins that are loaded at runtime—web search tools, database query tools, code execution sandboxes. These tool plugins may themselves have dependencies not covered by the main service dependency policy. Extend the dependency policy to cover tool plugins: each tool that is part of the approved tool registry (see the self-service tooling topics) should have its own dependency list reviewed as part of the tool approval process. A tool approved for production use implies that its dependencies are also reviewed and approved for the contexts in which the tool will be invoked.

Runtime-loaded plugins present a unique challenge: their dependencies may not be declared in the main service's package manifest. Solve this through two mechanisms. First, require tool plugins to include a `plugin-manifest.yaml` that lists all dependencies, which is checked against the policy during tool approval. Second, use a sandboxed execution environment for tool plugins—a Docker container or WebAssembly sandbox—that restricts the plugin's ability to load arbitrary dependencies at runtime. The sandbox boundary enforces that only declared, approved dependencies are available to the plugin, regardless of what the plugin code attempts to import.
Metrics and Policy Effectiveness

Measure the effectiveness of the dependency policy using four metrics. First, time to approve new dependencies: the average number of business days from approval request submission to decision. A long approval time (>10 days) indicates process bottlenecks that motivate teams to bypass the policy. Track this metric weekly and escalate to the platform team lead if it exceeds the 5-day SLA. Second, policy violation rate: the number of CI failures due to unapproved dependencies per week across all agent services. A declining trend indicates improving awareness and developer education; a rising trend may indicate the policy needs simplified documentation or better developer tooling.

Third, CVE response time: for each CVE affecting an approved package, the time from CVE publication to policy update specifying the safe minimum version. Track this metric per CVE severity level—critical CVEs (CVSS ≥9.0) should trigger policy updates within 4 hours; high severity within 24 hours; medium severity within one week. Fourth, dependency coverage: the percentage of dependencies used across all agent services that are explicitly listed in the approved policy (as opposed to unlisted but not yet blocked). A coverage below 80% indicates the policy catalog needs to catch up with current usage, creating an approval debt that should be resolved before the next audit.
Key Takeaways
An approved dependency policy limits the LLM library attack surface to a curated, security-reviewed set enforced automatically in CI.
Scope approvals to sub-packages with explicit use case documentation to prevent scope creep and transitive risk expansion.
Maintain both an approval process (for adding new libraries) and a CVE response process (for updating version constraints on known-vulnerable approved libraries).
Generate SBOMs for every deployment to maintain an auditable record of what libraries were in production at any point in time.
Protect the policy file with multi-reviewer branch rules and signed commits to prevent unauthorized policy relaxation.
Additional implementation considerations

Implementing these practices effectively requires understanding both the technical and organizational context in which they will operate. Teams at different stages of AI maturity will apply these practices differently: a team that has operated AI systems in production for two years has a richer base of operational experience to inform their implementation choices than a team deploying their first agent. Neither team should simply adopt a template; both should evaluate each practice against their specific risk profile, operational constraints, and team capabilities.

Cross-functional collaboration is essential for sustainable implementation. Engineering, AI safety, product, and SRE teams all have legitimate interests in how these practices are designed and enforced. Practices designed without input from the teams who must live with them tend to be either too abstract to apply consistently or too prescriptive to accommodate the real variability in situations they will encounter. Invest in the collaborative design process upfront; the time spent building shared understanding saves time in adoption and reduces the resistance that comes from practices imposed without consultation.

Measurement is the foundation of continuous improvement. Each practice described here has an associated metric: review cycle time, DoD completion rate, eval pass rate, incident rate, checklist failure distribution. Teams that track these metrics and review them regularly can identify whether their implementations are achieving their intended effects, where friction is causing workarounds, and where investment in tooling or capacity would provide the highest return. Practices that are not measured are not improved; they either remain static while the environment changes around them or gradually decay as their original purpose is forgotten.

Finally, recognize that the practices described here are means, not ends. The ends are: agent capabilities that work reliably, behave safely, can be operated and maintained by the team, and create genuine value for users. Every practice should be evaluated against these outcomes. When a practice is creating overhead without demonstrably contributing to these outcomes, it should be reformed or replaced. When a practice is clearly preventing incidents or improving quality, it should be maintained even when it creates friction. The discipline of outcome-oriented engineering process management is what separates teams that maintain effective practices long-term from teams whose practices gradually become compliance exercises.
